coredata.dc:description: Luminescent solar concentrators (LSCs) have important application in building integrated photovoltaic. CsPbX3 (X = Cl, Br, I) perovskite nanocrystals possessing tunable band gap energies, large absorption coefficients, narrow band photoluminescence (PL), and high photoluminescence quantum yield (PL QY), have great potential application in LSCs. However, instability of perovskite nanocrystals in ambient environment still limits their practical application. Herein, as a proof-of-concept experiment, CsPbBr3 nanocrystal-embedded glass sheets with various geometric factors are investigated as LSCs. Due to the wide absorption spectral range, high PL QY, and narrow PL, optical efficiency of 6.17% (2 × 2 cm2) is achieved from LSCs based on CsPbBr3 nanocrystal-embedded glass sheets. Importantly, the stability tests show that the glass matrix can effectively protect CsPbBr3 nanocrystals from eroding of ambient environment. These results show that CsPbBr3 nanocrystal-embedded glasses are promising materials for LSCs.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Images of PG and heat-treated glasses recorded (a) under normal room lighting condition and (b) 365 nm UV light illumination. (c) XRD patterns recorded from PG and heat-treated glasses. The numbers in the figure present the heat-treatment temperature and duration.

(a) TEM image of glass heat-treated at 470 °C for 9 h, (b) HR-TEM image of one nanocrystal formed in the glass and (c) the FFT image of the nanocrystal shown in (b). Inset in (a) is the histogram of diameters of CsPbBr3 nanocrystals.

(a) Absorption spectra, (b) PL spectra, (c) PL decay curves, and (d) PL QY of CsPbBr3 nanocrystal-embedded glasses. PL spectra are recorded with 365 nm light as excitation PL spectra are normalized and vertically shifted for comparison. PL QYs are measured with 365 nm light as excitation. Inset in (c) shows the enlarged PL decay curves in the range of 0–150 ns.

(a) Photo-image of CsPbBr3 nanocrystal-embedded glass sheet with a size of 2.0 × 2.0 × 0.11 cm3 taken under daylight condition. CsPbBr3 nanocrystals are precipitated in the glass upon heat-treatment at 460 °C for 3 h. (b) 3D schematic illustration of the LSC setup used in this work. Inset in (b) shows the optical image of the setup recorded under solar simulation illumination. (c) Absorption and PL spectra of CsPbBr3 nanocrystal-embedded glass sheet with a size of 2.0 × 2.0 × 0.11 cm3. The PL spectrum is recorded upon 365 nm light excitation and normalized to the maximum of the absorption shoulder. (d) Optical efficiency of LSCs consisting of CsPbBr3 nanocrystal-embedded glass sheets obtained using analytical modeling (dashed lines) and Monte Carlo ray-tracing simulation (solid circles). The open circles are experimentally measured values from CsPbBr3 nanocrystal-embedded glass sheet with PL QY of 73%. (e) J-V characteristic curves of LSCs with different geometric factors. Thickness of CsPbBr3 nanocrystal-embedded glass sheets used in (d) and (e) are polished from 0.11 to 0.024 cm, and PL QYs of 55, 73, and 90% are assumed for the analytical model and Monte Carlo ray-tracing simulation. (f) PCE of LSCs. Inset in (f) shows the J-V curve under direct illumination of simulated solar light (100 mW/cm2) of commercial single crystalline silicon photovoltaic cell (2 × 1 cm2) used in this work. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: CsPbBr3 nanocrystal-embedded glasses for luminescent solar concentrators

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Silicate Materials for Architectures, Wuhan University of Technology, 122 Luoshi Road, Hongshan, Wuhan, 430070, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Silicate Materials for Architectures

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Silicate Materials for Architectures, Wuhan University of Technology, 122 Luoshi Road, Hongshan, Wuhan, 430070, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Luminescent solar concentrators (LSCs) have important application in building integrated photovoltaic. CsPbX3 (X = Cl, Br, I) perovskite nanocrystals possessing tunable band gap energies, large absorption coefficients, narrow band photoluminescence (PL), and high photoluminescence quantum yield (PL QY), have great potential application in LSCs. However, instability of perovskite nanocrystals in ambient environment still limits their practical application. Herein, as a proof-of-concept experiment, CsPbBr3 nanocrystal-embedded glass sheets with various geometric factors are investigated as LSCs. Due to the wide absorption spectral range, high PL QY, and narrow PL, optical efficiency of 6.17% (2 × 2 cm2) is achieved from LSCs based on CsPbBr3 nanocrystal-embedded glass sheets. Importantly, the stability tests show that the glass matrix can effectively protect CsPbBr3 nanocrystals from eroding of ambient environment. These results show that CsPbBr3 nanocrystal-embedded glasses are promising materials for LSCs.

ce:section.ce:para: Luminescent solar concentrators (LSCs) have attracted a lot of attention due to their potential applications in building integrated photovoltaics, “smart” windows, net-zero energy buildings and utility and space solar [1–5]. Luminescent materials, which possess large absorption coefficients, high photoluminescence quantum yield (PL QY) and large Stokes shift, are preferred to achieve higher concentrating effect of solar energy and suppress self-absorption and quenching loss [4,6–8]. Among various luminescent materials, organic dyes, rare-earth ions, and quantum dots (QDs) have been used for LSCs [9–11]. Due to the widely adjustable characteristics and near-unity PL QY, organic dyes such as Lumogen F Red 305, have been utilized in large area and flexible LSCs [12,13]. Rare-earth ions with f-f transition, which exhibit narrow absorption and photoluminescence (PL) bands, are also one of the ideal fluorescent materials in LSCs [14,15]. However, wide applications of these organic dyes and rare-earth ions in LSCs are mainly restricted by their chemical stabilities and small absorption spectral range, respectively. Compared with organic dyes and rare-earth ions, colloidal QDs such as CdS, CdSe, CuInS2, etc. are promising candidates for LSCs, due to their size-dependent tunable bandgap, large absorption coefficients, and high PL QY [16–19]. Optical power efficiency and power conversion efficiency (PCE) as high as 6.8% and 2.94% have been achieved from LSC-based CuInS2 nanocrystals, respectively [19,20]. In addition, earth-abundant silicon QDs and carbon QDs (C dots) have also been considered as important alternatives for LSCs due to their wide PL band, high PL QY, and intrinsic non-toxicity [21–23]. Zhao et al. fabricate large area (up to 225 cm2) LSCs based on colloidal C dots with PL QY of ≈65% and Stokes shift of ≈0.53 eV by drop-casting on glass substrate [24]. Under natural sunlight illumination (60 mW/cm2), these C dots-based LSCs exhibit PCE of 1.1% and optical efficiency of 2.2%. These features indicate that QDs are promising luminescent materials for LSCs.

Recently, CsPbX3 (X = Cl, Br, I) perovskite QDs have demonstrated their great potentials toward high energy ray detectors, light emitting diodes (LEDs) and lasing applications [25–27]. CsPbX3 perovskite QDs have size- and compositional-dependent bandgap energies, large absorption cross section, and large defect tolerance, making it possible to utilize wide spectral range of solar irradiation and achieve high PL QY [28,29]. Thus, CsPbX3 perovskite QDs are also potential alternatives toward LSCs. In recent years, LSCs based on lead halide perovskite QDs have been intensively studied (More performance comparisons are shown in Table S1). Using mixed halide perovskite QDs, LSCs responsive in 300–650 nm are developed [30]. A tandem LSC based on C dots and mixed halide perovskite is constructed [31]. The stability is dramatically improved because the first layer C dots filters 75% UV power. 0D Cs4PbBr6 nanocrystals show great potentials on stable and efficient large-scale LSCs, and preliminarily realize the device integration [32]. Recently, lead-free double perovskite nanocrystals LSC based on Bi-doped Cs2Ag0.4Na0.6InCl6 with large Stokes shift up to 1.45 eV is illustrated by Zdražil et al. for the first time [33]. Doping transition metal ions such as Mn2+ and rare-earth ions such as Yb3+ have also been utilized to reduce the reabsorption effect of perovskite nanocrystals [34–36]. Though LSCs with a large Stokes shift are achieved in doped CsPbCl3 nanocrystals, they usually suffer from lower PL QY and narrower absorption region. Meinardi et al. [35] point out that these nanocrystals with wide band gap (UV cutoff edge around 395 nm) are not suitable for practical LSC due to the strong superposition with matrix absorption and the weak harvest on solar spectrum (less than 5%). Perovskite nanoplates with quantum confined layer structure demonstrate another strategy to increase the Stokes shift and achieve high performance LSCs [37,38]. Benefitting from steep absorption cut-off edge and narrow full width at half maximum (FWHM) of photoluminescence band, perovskite QDs exhibit relatively low absorption at emission peak even with a small Stokes shift [39]. Cubic FAPbBr3 and CsPbI3 colloidal nanocrystals with near unity PL QYs are synthesized by Deng and coworkers [40,41]. LSCs based on these cubic perovskite nanocrystals exhibit wide absorption of solar spectrum and significantly suppress the quenching loss. However, LSC-based lead halide perovskite QDs still suffer from the several drawbacks due to the relatively weak chemical stability [41,42] and agglomeration-induced quenching of PL [43]. As a result, it is necessary to find proper encapsulation and dispersion methods to maintain the chemical stabilities and high PL QY of CsPbX3 perovskite nanocrystals for the potential application as LSCs.

Incorporation of CsPbX3 perovskite QDs into glasses can keep them from harsh environment, because glasses are inert and amorphous materials [44,45]. CsPbX3 perovskite nanocrystals have been precipitated in phosphate glasses, borosilicate glasses, borogermante glasses, tellurite glasses through conventional melt-quenching and subsequent thermal treatment [46–50]. Chemical composition, average size, and PL QY of CsPbX3 perovskite nanocrystals can be tuned by adjusting the chemical composition of the glass, thermal treatment conditions (such as heat-treatment temperature and duration) [51,52]. It has been shown that incorporation of CsPbX3 perovskite nanocrystals in glasses do significantly improve their chemical stabilities, thermal stabilities and resistance toward light irradiation [53,54]. For CsPbX3 nanocrystal-embedded boro-silicate glasses, PL intensity maintain 90% after immersing in water for 30 days, and nearly 100% upon irradiation by 450 nm light from LED for 50 h [53]. For CsPbX3 nanocrystal-embedded glasses, PL of CsPbX3 nanocrystals exhibit reversible dependence on temperature, and almost completely recover to their original states after multiple heating-cooling cycles [54]. In addition, for CsPbX3 nanocrystal-embedded glasses, these nanocrystals are isolated from each other by the amorphous glass matrix, reducing agglomeration induced quenching of PL and improving the overall PL QY [55]. These features indicate that CsPbX3 nanocrystal-embedded glasses could potentially applied in the field of LSCs.

In this work, LSCs based on CsPbX3 nanocrystal-embedded glasses are investigated. CsPbBr3 nanocrystals are precipitated in glasses through conventional melt-quenching and subsequent thermal treatment. Effects of PL QYs and geometric factors (G factors) of CsPbX3 nanocrystal-embedded glass sheets are investigated experimentally and numerically using analytical modeling and Monte Carlo ray-tracing analysis. Using these CsPbBr3 nanocrystal-embedded glass sheet, LSC with optical efficiency of 6.17% (G ≈ 4.5) and PCE of 0.57% is achieved.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CsPbBr3 perovskite nanocrystal-embedded glasses
ce:section.ce:para: Precursor glass (PG) with nominal composition of 50.00B2O3-27.38ZnO-4.76Al2O3-2.38CaO-3.57Cs2O-7.15KBr-4.76PbBr2 (in mol%) was prepared using melt-quenching method. Chemical powders with high purities of >99.9% were properly weighted and mixed thoroughly in an agate mortar before melting in alumina crucible at 1100 °C for 20 min. The glass melt was poured onto a preheated copper mold and pressed with one copper plate for quenching and forming. The quenched glass was swiftly transferred to a muffle furnace for annealing at 350 °C for 2 h to release the thermal stress. Glass thus obtained was cut into 2 × 2 cm2 squares for further treatment. CsPbBr3 nanocrystals were precipitated in glass through heat treatment in muffle furnace at 460 or 470 °C for 3, 6, or 9 h, respectively.

The PG was heat treated at 460 °C for 3 h in a muffle furnace, and then cool down to room temperature. The heat-treated specimen was polished down to a size of 2 × 2 × 0.11 cm3. All six surfaces of the specimens were polished to an optical grade when LSC thickness was altered each time. A commercial crystalline silicon PV cell with a size of 2 × 1 cm2 was coupled to the glass edge size with covering excessive area by black tape.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded using D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer (Bruker, Germany) with Cu Kα radiation (40 kV, 40 mA) in a range from 10 to 60° and a scanning rate of 2°/min. Microstructure was recorded using a transmission electron microscope (TEM, F200S, Talos, USA) with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Raman spectra in the range of 50–1000 cm-1 is recorded using a laser confocal Raman spectroscopy (LabRam HR Evolution, HORIBA JOBIN YVON, France) equipped with a charge coupled device (CCD) detector and 633 nm laser (30 mW) as the excitation source. Absorption spectra were measured using UV–vis spectrophotometer (Lambda 750S, PerkinElmer, USA). PL spectra were measured using a home-made PL measurement system consisting of light source, mechanical chopper, monochromators, lock-in amplifier, and photodetector. Optical-path-dependent PL spectra were recorded by adjusting the excitation beam position, whereas fixing the positions of polished glass specimens and detectors. PL decay curves of CsPbBr3 nanocrystal-embedded glass specimens were recorded using time correlative single photon counting (TCSPC) method with 1040 nm fs laser as excitation source and the excitation power density was maintained at 40 mW/cm2. PL QY was measured by a UV–NIR quantum yield spectrometer (Quantaurus-QY Plus, Hamamatsu, Japan), and the uncertainty in the measured PL QY is 1%. Refractive index of the CsPbBr3 nanocrystal-embedded glass was measured using ellipsometer (SE-VM, Wuhan Eoptics Technology Co. Ltd.). The external optical efficiency and PCE were measured under AM 1.5G irradiation (100 mW/cm2) from a calibrated solar simulator (Oriel Sol2A, NEWPORT, USA) with a commercial monocrystalline Si cell mounted on the edge of LSC. J-V curves were measured using PVIV-412V (Newport, USA) under simulator solar spectrum (AM 1.5G, 100 mW/cm2) calibrated by a standard Si cell, and collected by a Keithley 2440 source meter.

The glass is prepared using conventional melt-quenching method as used in our previous work [46,48], and CsPbBr3 nanocrystals are precipitated in the glasses using thermal treatment method. The PG (i.e., as-annealed glass without heat treatment) specimen is transparent and colorless (Fig. 1 ). Upon heat-treatment at 460 or 470 °C for 3, 6, or 9 h (for simplicity, the heat treatment condition is abbreviated as 46003, etc.), these glass specimens change into yellow color and exhibit weak green emission under normal room lighting condition (Fig. 1a). The green emission observed under normal room lighting condition indicates that CsPbBr3 nanocrystals with high PL QY are precipitated in the glasses. Upon 365 nm UV light illumination, the PG does not show any visible emission, whereas all of the heat-treated glasses exhibit bright green emission (Fig. 1b). Such changes in color and green emission of the heat-treated glasses are similar to previous work [48,56], indicating the precipitation of CsPbBr3 nanocrystals in the glasses upon heat-treatment. XRD patterns recorded from PG specimen and heat-treated specimens are shown in Fig. 1c. For the PG specimen, only broad halos are observed, indicating that PG specimen is amorphous without detectable crystalline phase. When these glasses are heat treated at 460 °C for 3 or 6 h, no diffraction peaks from crystalline phases can be observed, which is most probably related to the low crystallinity and small size of the CsPbBr3 nanocrystals formed in the glasses. When the glass is heat treated at 460 °C for 9 h, several weak diffraction peaks (marked by asterisks in Fig. 1c) are observed. These diffraction peaks become prominent when the glasses are heat treated at 470 °C. These diffraction peaks are consistent with diffraction of CsPbBr3 crystal (JCPDF no.: 75–0412), indicating that cubic-structured CsPbBr3 nanocrystals are precipitated in glasses upon heat treatment. Using Scherrer equation [57] and (110), (200), (210), (220) diffraction peaks, average diameters of CsPbBr3 nanocrystals formed in glasses heat treated at 470 °C for 3, 6, and 9 h are calculated to be 12.5 ± 0.3, 14.1 ± 0.3, and 13.3 ± 0.6 nm, respectively. These radii are comparable with the exciton Bohr radius (7 nm) of bulk CsPbBr3 crystal [26]. It also needs to be pointed out that no diffraction peak at 12.5°, which is typical for Cs4PbBr6 nanocrystalline phase, can be observed from XRD patterns recorded from these specimens [42,58], indicating that no Cs4PbBr6 nanocrystals are formed in these specimens.

Precipitation of CsPbBr3 nanocrystals in glass heat treated at 470 °C for 9 h is further confirmed using high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM, Fig. 2 ). Upon heat treatment, nearly spherical nanocrystals with average diameter of 15.2 nm and size dispersion of 12% are randomly distributed in the glass (Fig. 2a). The HR-TEM image of one nanocrystal shows that nanocrystal formed in the glass is highly crystalline with clear lattice fringe (Fig. 2b). The crystal plane distance calculated from Fig. 2b is found to be 2.39 Å, consistent with the (211) crystal place distance of CsPbBr3 crystals. In addition, the fast Fourier transformation (FFT) image (Fig. 2c) of the nanocrystal shown in Fig. 2b also confirms the cubic structure of the nanocrystals formed in the glasses. Both XRD patterns and the TEM analysis results confirm that nanocrystals formed in the heat-treated glasses are CsPbBr3 nanocrystals.

Raman spectra recorded from PG and heat-treated samples (Fig. S1) also confirm the precipitation of CsPbBr3 perovskite nanocrystals in glass. Raman peaks at ≈ 310 cm-1 appear and grow in intensity with the increase in heat-treatment temperature or prolongation in heat-treatment duration (Fig. S1), which is related to the motion of cesium cations, typical of cubic CsPbBr3 [59]. In addition, another peak at 70 cm-1 is observed (Fig. S1), and this peak also grow in intensity with the increase in heat-treatment temperature or prolongation in heat-treatment duration. This peak is associated with the distorted [PbBr6]4- octahedron [60]. Both XRD patterns (Fig. 1c) and Raman spectra (Fig. S1) show that CsPbBr3 nanocrystals are precipitated in glasses upon heat treatment. In addition to these two Raman peaks, another two Raman peaks at 770 and 808 cm-1 are observed (Fig. S1). These two Raman peaks are assigned to the vibration modes of six-member borate rings with one BO4 tetrahedron and boroxol rings, respectively [61,62]. Upon heat treatment, these two Raman peaks do not show much changes, indicating that glass matrices are stable during heat treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of CsPbBr3 nanocrystals glasses
ce:section.ce:para: Optical properties of the heat-treated glasses are shown in Fig. 3 . The PG is highly transparent in the visible spectral with an absorption edge at ≈ 350 nm, and neither any additional absorption peaks nor shoulders is observed in the spectral range between 350 and 570 nm, indicating that no CsPbBr3 nanocrystals is present in the PG (Fig. 3a). This observation is consistent with the colorless feature shown in Fig. 1a and XRD pattern recorded from PG specimen (Fig. 1c). However, upon heat treatment at elevated temperatures, absorption shoulders or absorption edges appear in the visible range, consistent with those shown in Fig. 1. When the glasses are heat treated at 460 °C for 3 or 6 h, 470 °C for 3 h, excitonic absorption peaks induced by the CsPbBr3 nanocrystals in glasses are observed (Fig. 3a). When the glasses are heat treated at 460 °C for 9 h, 470 °C for 6 or 9 h, absorption of these heat-treated glasses saturates and only absorption edges are observed (Fig. 3a). Both absorption shoulders and absorption edges shift toward long wavelength with the increase in temperature or prolongation of heat treatment. These absorption spectra show that number densities and the corresponding absorption coefficients of CsPbBr3 nanocrystal-embedded glasses are strongly dependent on heat-treatment temperature and duration, which increase with the increase in heat-treatment temperature or prolongation of heat-treatment. PL spectra of these CsPbBr3 nanocrystals are recorded upon 365 nm light excitation (Fig. 3b). Similar to those shown in Fig. 3a, peak wavelengths of PL bands shift from 518 to 525 nm, with a slight change in the spectral line-shape as the heat-treatment temperature or duration increases, indicating the gradual growth and possible coalescence of CsPbBr3 nanocrystals in the glass specimens [63,64]. At the meantime, full width at half maximum (FWHM) of these PL bands gradually increases from 20 to 28 nm. Such narrow FWHM of PL bands is comparable with those observed from colloidal perovskite nanocrystals [28], consistent with the narrow size distribution of CsPbBr3 nanocrystals shown in the inset of Fig. 2a. Compared with the absorption of these CsPbBr3 nanocrystal-embedded glasses, Stokes shift of the PL bands are found to be ≈ 66, ≈65, ≈12 meV for glasses heat treated at 460 °C for 3, 6, and 9 h, and ≈60, ≈36, and ≈8 meV for glasses heat treated at 470 °C for 3, 6, and 9 h, respectively. These values show that CsPbBr3 nanocrystal-embedded glasses heat treated for 3 and 6 h are more suitable for spectral conversion application due to the smaller reabsorption effect, when compared with CsPbBr3 nanocrystal-embedded glasses heat treated for 9 h.

Upon 1040 nm fs laser excitation, PL decay curves of these CsPbBr3 nanocrystal-embedded glasses are recorded (Fig. 3c). The inset in Fig. 3c shows enlarged PL decay curves in the range of 0–150 ns. All of these PL decay curves can be well fitted using biexponential function (Equation S(1)), indicating that there are two recombination processes for these CsPbBr3 nanocrystals in glasses. For CsPbBr3 nanocrystals in glasses heat-treated at 460 °C, the fast decay component increases ≈6 to ≈11 and ≈13 ns, and slow decay component increases from ≈24 to ≈86 and ≈245 ns when the heat-treatment duration increases from 3 to 6 and 9 h, respectively (Fig. S2). For CsPbBr3 nanocrystals in glasses heat treated at 470 °C, the fast decay component decreases from ≈30 to ≈27 and ≈9 ns, and slow decay component changes from ≈145 to ≈176 and ≈144 ns when the heat-treatment duration increases from 3 to 6 and 9 h, respectively (Fig. S2). Average lifetimes calculated using Equation S(2), are found to be ≈ 22, ≈79, ≈241, ≈136, ≈167, and ≈141 ns for CsPbBr3 nanocrystal-embedded glasses heat treated at 460 °C (for 3, 6, and 9 h) and 470 °C (for 3, 6, and 9 h), respectively. All these time constants are comparable with those observed from chemically synthesized CsPbBr3 nanocrystals [26,28,65]. PL QY of these CsPbBr3 nanocrystal-embedded glasses is strongly dependent on heat-treatment conditions (Fig. 3d). For glasses heat treated at 460 °C, PL QY decreases from 81 ± 1% to 73 ± 1% and 53 ± 1% when the heat-treatment duration increases from 3 to 6 and 9 h, respectively. For glasses heat treated at 470 °C, PL QY is much lower compared with those heat treated at 460 °C, and it decreases from 42 ± 1% to 15 ± 1% and 18 ± 1% when the heat-treatment duration increases from 3 to 6 and 9 h, respectively. It has to be noted that these PL QY values are measured from 1 mm3 cubic specimens to reduce the effect of reabsorption. In addition, with the increase in heat-treatment duration and temperature, absorption coefficients of CsPbBr3 nanocrystal-embedded glass gradually increase or saturate (Fig. 3a), due to the gradual increase in number densities of CsPbBr3 nanocrystals in glasses. Consequently, reabsorption of PL from CsPbBr3 nanocrystals in glasses is enhanced, resulting in the lengthening in PL lifetimes and reduction in PL QY. These results indicate that a proper concentration of CsPbBr3 nanocrystals in glasses is necessary to realize efficient spectral conversion.

Stabilities of CsPbBr3 nanocrystal-embedded glass against higher energy photon illumination, water attack, and thermal cycling are investigated (Fig. S3), taking glass sample heat treated at 460 °C for 3 h as one typical example. To test the photo-stability, these CsPbBr3 nanocrystal-embedded glass samples are irradiated by one 3 W blue LED. The irradiation power density on the sample surface is maintained at 15 mW/cm2, and environmental temperature is kept at ≈ 25 ± 1 °C, and environmental humidity is kept at ≈ 40 ± 3%. Upon continuously irradiation for 7 days, no changes in PL intensities are observed (black squares in Fig. S3a). Stability against water attack is verified by immersing the glass sample into water for 7 days. The residual PL intensity still remains more than 96%, compared with the original PL intensity (red circles in Fig. S9a). Inset in Fig. S3a shows the photographs of glass sample in water recorded under 365 nm light illumination. These results show that CsPbBr3 nanocrystal-embedded glass sample is highly stable against light irradiation and water attack. Thermal stability of the CsPbBr3 nanocrystal-embedded glass sample is evaluated using thermal cycling experiment (Fig. S3b). PL intensity of the glass sample decreases upon heating from 25 to 150 °C, and then increases to its original value upon cooling from 150 to 25 °C (Fig. S3b). After five thermal cycling experiments, PL intensity recovers its original state. These results show that CsPbBr3 nanocrystal-embedded glass samples are highly stable and promising for practical applications such LSCs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: LSCs based on CsPbBr3 nanocrystal-embedded glasses
ce:section.ce:para: As a proof-of-concept experiment, LSCs based on CsPbBr3 nanocrystal-embedded glasses are fabricated. Under sunlight illumination, green color emission is observed from the CsPbBr3 nanocrystal-embedded glass sheet, clearly demonstrating the luminescent solar concentrating effect (Fig. 4 a). Due to the refractive index differences between glass and air (nglass > nair), the “CsPbBr3 nanocrystal-embedded glass sheet” is natural planar optical waveguide device. Considering the absorption coefficient and PL QY (Fig. 3), CsPbBr3 nanocrystal-embedded glass sheets with a size of 2 × 2 cm2 and different thickness obtained upon heat treatment at 460 °C for 3 h are prepared to fabricate LSCs, as illustrated in Fig. 4b. The commercial crystalline silicon photovoltaic cell is closely mounted on one of the edges of the glass sheet, and the excessive region of the cell is covered by black tape to avoid the effect of scattered light. The other three edge surfaces of the glass sheet are sealed with highly absorbing black tapes together with a homemade clamping device. Absorption and PL spectra recorded from 0.11 mm-thick glass sheet are shown in Fig. 4c. PL band recorded from the glass sheet centers at 521 nm with FWHM of 20 nm (Fig. 4c). Stokes shift of PL from CsPbBr3 QDs-embedded glass sheet is ≈ 65 meV, which is smaller than those recorded from the mixed halide perovskite QDs and doped perovskite QDs [30]. Such small Stokes shift may induce relatively strong reabsorption of the PL, however, the narrow FWHM of the PL band may reduce the reabsorption effect. When normalize the PL spectrum relatively to the maximum of absorption shoulder, the overlapped area (grey part, representing the possible reabsorption of the PL) is found to be relatively small (Fig. 4c). Using the setup shown in Fig. S4, optical path-dependent PL spectra are recorded and shown in Fig. S5. For the PL spectrum recorded from the sample edge (optical path of 0 mm), it overlaps strongly with the absorption spectrum, leading to very efficient reabsorption effect. With the increase in optical path, PL from the excited CsPbBr3 nanocrystals in glasses can be absorbed by CsPbBr3 nanocrystals located in the sample volume between the excitation spot and sample edge. This strong reabsorption significantly changes the shape of the PL band by reducing its intensity at short wavelength side, and redshift the PL peak position (Fig. S5). Eventually, with the increase in optical path, peak wavelength of these PL bands gradually stabilizes to 536 nm and FWHM of these PL bands stabilize to 15 nm (Fig. S5c). Comparing with the absorption shoulder, the overlapped area gradually reduces when optical path is larger than 18 mm (Fig. S5a). These features show that luminescent concentrating effect is achievable, indicating that CsPbBr3 nanocrystal-embedded glasses are promising for application as LSC.

Optical efficiencies are recorded to illustrate the performance of these LSCs based on CsPbBr3 nanocrystal-embedded glasses with different thicknesses (Fig. 4d). The optical efficiency is defined as the photons emitted from edges divided by the photons impinging on the LSC top surface. When LSC is mounted with a photovoltaic cell, the optical efficiency (

The commercial single crystalline silicon photovoltaic cell (2 × 1 cm2) is tested before mounting into the LSCs, and the values of short-circuit current density (Jsc), open-circuit voltage (Voc), fill factor (FF) and PCE are measured to be 32.03 mA/cm2, 0.58 V, 71.56% and 14.11%, respectively (The J-V curve is shown as inset in Fig. 4f). LSCs with different G factors in the range of 4.5–20.8 are prepared by gradually reducing the thickness of the CsPbBr3 nanocrystal-embedded glass sheets (2 × 2 cm2) from 1.1 to 0.24 mm. With G factor of ≈4.5, optical efficiency of the LSC is measured to be 6.17% (open circles in Fig. 4d). The optical efficiency gradually decreases to 2.10% when the G factor increases to ≈20.8 (open circles in Fig. 4d). Optical efficiencies of these LSCs are also analyzed using the simplified analytical model [39] (dashed lines in Fig. 4d, details of the analytical modelling is shown in Supporting Information). The analytical modeling predicts that the optical efficiency decreases with the increase in G factor of the LSCs, and monotonically decreases with the reduction in PL QY of the CsPbBr3 nanocrystal-embedded glass sheets. In addition, the optical efficiency experimentally measured from LSCs using the CsPbBr3 nanocrystal-embedded glass sheet with PL QY of 73% (Fig. S6) is found to be comparable with the optical efficiency predicted from the analytical model (Fig. 4d). Using the analytical modeling, the internal quantum efficiency (IQE) is also calculated using CsPbBr3 nanocrystal-embedded glass sheets with PL QYs of 55, 73, and 90% (Fig. S7). Again, the experimentally measured IQEs from the CsPbBr3 nanocrystal-embedded glass sheets with PL QY of 73% and G factors in the range of 4.5–20.8 are comparable with the values predicted using the analytical model. However, slight deviations of optical efficiencies and IQEs (Figs. 4d and S7) are found from glass sheets with larger G factors. Such deviation is induced by the suppressed reabsorption through the thickness (perpendicular direction to the surface of the glass sheet) with the increasement in G factor (i.e., reduce in thickness of the glass sheet).

To understand the reabsorption phenomena in CsPbBr3 nanocrystal-embedded glass sheet, photons evolving in the glass sheets with different G factors are simulated using Monte Carlo ray-tracing simulation [66] (Details are shown in Supporting Information). CsPbBr3 nanocrystal-embedded glass sheets with PL QYs of 55, 73 and 90% are used for the simulation. Optical efficiencies of CsPbBr3 nanocrystal-embedded glass sheets with different G factors are obtained through the simulation (solid circles in Fig. 4d). These simulated optical efficiencies are comparable with those obtained through analytical modeling. These results show that such high optical efficiencies measured from the LSCs are reasonable. Upon solar light illumination, photons generated in CsPbBr3 nanocrystal-embedded glass sheets can be collected at the edge contacting with photovoltaic solar cell, quenched by reabsorption, or escape from the glass sheets. Using Monte Carlo ray-tracing simulation, ratios of quenched, collected, and escaped photons are shown in Fig. S8. As the G factor increases, ratios of quenched and escaped photons increase, and ratio of collected photons gradually decreases. As the G factor increases, emitted photons are highly concentrated in the glass sheets, leading to enhanced reabsorption and subsequent reduction in ratio of collected photons (Figs. S8 and S9). Using the Monte-Carlo ray-tracing simulation, it is found that most of the collected photons experience 1–3 times reabsorption events (Fig. S9). With the increase in G factors, proportions of emitted photons experiencing multiple reabsorption events increase, especially for CsPbBr3 nanocrystal-embedded glass sheets with higher PL QYs (Fig. S9). With the increase in PL QYs of the CsPbBr3 nanocrystal-embedded glass sheets, ratios of escaped photons and collected photons increase, while ratio of quenched photons gradually decreases, when glass sheets with the same G factor are compared (Fig. S8). These results show that higher PL QYs and proper G factors are necessary to achieve more collected photons.

The overall PCE of LSC-PV system, defined as the ratio of the power from edge-mounted photovoltaic cells and the solar power irradiated on LSC top surface, is another key criterion to estimate the performance of LSC-PV system, shown in Equation (2) [67,68].

J-V characteristic curves and PCEs of LSCs based on CsPbBr3 nanocrystal-embedded glass sheets are recorded and shown in Fig. 4e and f. For clear comparison, one blank (without CsPbBr3 nanocrystals inside) aluminosilicate glass sheet with a size of 2 × 2 × 0.1 cm3 and refractive index of 1.50 at 520 nm is used as a reference, and the short-circuit current and PCE recorded with this blank glass sheet is 0.29 mA and 0.06%, respectively (Table S2). With the increase in G factors, the corresponding short-circuit current density gradually decrease from 0.49 mA/cm2 (G ≈ 4.5) to 0.17 mA/cm2 (G ≈ 20.8) (Fig. 4e). Open-circuit voltage monotonically decreases with the increase in G factors (Fig. 4e and Table S2). When the G factor of the CsPbBr3 nanocrystal-embedded glass sheet is increased from ≈4.5 to ≈20.8, PCEs gradually decrease from 0.57 to 0.16%. These results indicate that CsPbX3 perovskite nanocrystal-embedded glass are promising candidates as solar spectral conversion materials.

LSC based on CsPbBr3 nanocrystal-embedded glasses is demonstrated. These CsPbBr3 nanocrystals are precipitated inside glasses through conventional melt-quenching and subsequent thermal treatment. Spherical CsPbBr3 nanocrystals are nearly homogeneously precipitated in the glasses. Average diameters, absorption and PL properties of these CsPbBr3 nanocrystal-embedded glasses are adjusted by thermal treatment conditions, and PL QY as high as 73 ± 1% is realized. With the increase in optical path, peak wavelength and FWHM of PL bands gradually stabilize to 536 and 15 nm, respectively. The glass matrix almost maintains original PL after immersed in water or irradiated by UV light for one week, and multiple thermal cycling tests. Optical efficiency of 6.17% with G factor of ≈4.5 and PCE of 0.57% are achieved from LSCs based on CsPbBr3 nanocrystal-embedded glass sheets (2 × 2 cm2). These results reveal that CsPbBr3 nanocrystals glasses are promising for LSCs.
